q: Sound is a sequence of pressure waves which propagates through compressible media.
During their propagation, waves can be reflected, refracted or attenuated by the medium.
The speed of sound within a medium is independent of temperature.
Viscosity determines the rate at which sound is attenuated.
In many media, such as air or water, attenuation due to viscosity is extremely high.
The speed of sound will be the same for all different media.
Longitudinal sound waves travel faster in gases than they do in solids.
Young's modulus and the bulk modulus are elasticity properties of the medium.
The SI units of Young's modulus and the bulk modulus are $N \cdot m^{-2}$.
The minimum intensity of sound that can be heard is called the threshold of hearing.
The reference intensity value $I_0$ is taken as $10^{-10} W \cdot m^{-2}$.
The decibel ($dB$) is the unit used for measuring sound intensity.
For every $10 dB$ added to the sound level, the loudness of the sound is increased by 100 times.
Sound intensity is a direct measure of the power reaching an eardrum.
The intensity level of sound $\beta$ is measured relative to a reference intensity $I_0$.
